title: 5 Nehase — St. Philip the Apostle & The Assembly
narrative:
Nehase

St. Philip the Apostle (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Nonna, Mother of St. Gregory the Theologian

St. Eusignius the Martyr

On this fifth day of Nehase, the Church honors St. Philip the Apostle in his monthly commemoration — and for the Ethiopian Church this particular apostle carries a special warmth, for it was to Philip that the Holy Spirit said, 'Go and join yourself to this chariot,' sending him to meet the Ethiopian eunuch on the road to Gaza. That encounter, described in the eighth chapter of the Acts of the Apostles, is the pivotal moment in the Ethiopian Church's self-understanding: her apostolic lineage runs directly through Philip. He who explained the scroll of Isaiah to the royal treasurer of Queen Candace, and who baptized him in the roadside water, planted the seed that would become one of the oldest continuous Christian civilizations on earth. Philip is not merely an apostle in the Ethiopian calendar — he is, in a sense, the apostle.

St. Nonna, the mother of St. Gregory the Theologian, is commemorated today as a saint in her own right — not merely as the parent of a great figure but as a woman whose personal holiness was formative and whose intercessions were powerful. She prayed her husband Gregory the Elder into the faith and ultimately into the episcopate. She shaped the theological imagination of her son through the domestic church of their household. The tradition of the Ethiopian Orthodox Church honors mothers with great reverence, understanding that the family — the household church — is the first and most enduring school of sanctity. Nonna is the patron of mothers who pray without ceasing and who count their children's souls as their primary vocation.

St. Eusignius the Martyr was a soldier of the Roman army who had witnessed, reportedly, the Constantinian vision of the Cross before the Battle of the Milvian Bridge, and who spent the subsequent decades of his long life in faithful Christian service before being martyred in his extreme old age during the reign of Julian the Apostate. His story is remarkable for its breadth: a man who bridged the era of persecution and the era of imperial Christianity, who had seen the world change and who kept his faith through all the changes. He died not as a young idealist but as an old soldier who knew exactly what he was dying for.

Philip, Nonna, and Eusignius — apostle, mother, and soldier — gather on this fifth day of Nehase to present three essential forms of Christian witness in the world. Philip went to where the Scripture was being read without understanding, and provided the word of explanation that opened the way to baptism. Nonna worked within the hidden structures of family and prayer, reshaping the world through love rather than proclamation. Eusignius endured across the decades, outlasting emperors and empires, and died with his convictions intact. As the Church approaches the threshold of the great Filseta fast, the fast in honor of the Virgin Mother, the presence of Nonna among today's saints is quietly fitting — for it is maternal holiness that the Church will most intensely contemplate in the days ahead.
